title: The influence of strong coupling between single-photon source and spectral filter on photon statistics
authors: Ivan V. Panyukov, Evgeny S. Andrianov
abstract: One of the most common approaches for coupling optical single-photon sources and photonic integrated circuits is to use a cavity. The cavity acts as a spectral filter that distorts the light spectrum and changes its statistical properties. But in the general case one should take into account not only spectral filtering of light but also the spectral filter influence on the single-photon source dynamics. We build an effective analytical model for description of the cavity influence on the photon statistics of light emitted by the single-photon source as spectral filtering only. We show that this model correctly describes the photon statistics even in a strong-coupling regime between the single-photon source and the spectral filter. Our results can be useful for analytical modeling of photon statistics of quantum emitters strongly coupled to various electromagnetic interfaces.
summary: The paper shows that the impact of a cavity on a single-photon source's photon statistics can be accurately modeled as simple spectral filtering. This effective model remains valid even in the strong-coupling regime, except when extreme energy transfer rates trigger significant non-Markovian effects.
main_problem: The paper investigates whether the strong coupling between a single-photon source and a cavity (acting as a spectral filter) significantly alters the photon statistics beyond simple spectral filtering, specifically regarding the influence of the filter on the source's internal dynamics.
main_result: The authors demonstrate that an effective analytical model treating the cavity solely as a Lorentz spectral filter is accurate for most parameter regimes, even in strong coupling, provided that cavity-induced non-Markovian effects do not dominate.
method: The study uses a three-way comparison between a rigorous numerical Lindblad master equation approach, an effective analytical model with modified relaxation rates, and a simplified model that neglects the filter's influence on the source dynamics.
model_or_system: The system consists of a two-level system (TLS) undergoing incoherent pumping, coupled to a single-mode cavity via the Jaynes-Cummings Hamiltonian. The dynamics are described by a Lindblad master equation incorporating TLS decay, pure dephasing, cavity decay, and incoherent pumping.
key_observables: The second-order autocorrelation function g2(0) of the cavity emission.
important_parameters: Rabi constant (Omega), TLS decay rate (gamma_diss), cavity linewidth (gamma_a), pure dephasing rate (gamma_deph), pumping rate (gamma_pump), and the efficiency parameter (beta) representing the ratio of energy transfer to losses.
main_assumptions: The cavity mode is initially vacuum; the cavity acts as a Lorentz spectral filter; the TLS is incoherently pumped; the cavity frequency is resonant with the TLS transition frequency.
figures_summary: Figure 3 demonstrates the validity of the analytical approach for Rabi frequencies up to 100 times the TLS decay rate.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the problem of coupling single-photon sources to cavities, presents the theoretical framework (Hamiltonian and Master Equation), develops the effective analytical model, compares the three different modeling approaches across various coupling regimes, and identifies the limits of the effective model's validity.
why_interesting: This paper is highly relevant to researchers in quantum optics and open quantum systems as it provides a simplified analytical tool for predicting photon statistics in integrated photonic circuits, while precisely defining the boundaries where non-Markovian dynamics necessitate more complex models.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. Second-order autocorrelation function depending on the cavity linewidth γa and the Rabi constant Ω. (a) g(2)(0) calculated in the framework of the full quantum-mechanical model, including the TLS and the spectral filter. (b) g(2) eff (0) calculated via Eq. (26).
FIG. 2. Relative difference between g(2)(0) and g(2) eff (0).
FIG. 3. Second-order autocorrelation function calculated rigorously, neglecting the spectral filter influence, and via the effective model, at different values of Ω.
